main_damage_causes: The trial scene's emotional core - Barty Jr.'s desperate pleading, claimed innocence, and the father-son tragedy - is replaced with defiance and villainy, destroying the moral ambiguity and the thematic weight of Crouch Sr.'s rejection of his own child.

The Veritaserum confession is gutted, removing all backstory: his mother's sacrifice, his imprisonment, his years under the Imperius Curse, and his fanatical motivation - leaving his arc without explanation or emotional grounding.
The tongue-flick tic is used as a reductive villain shorthand from his first appearance, collapsing a complex, tragic figure into a one-note sinister presence throughout the film.

damaging_scenes.scene: Film Scene 2 - Pensieve trial: Barty Jr.'s reaction to sentencing
damaging_scenes.book_baseline: Barty Jr. pleads desperately and emotionally with his father, crying out 'Father, I didn't!', 'Don't send me back to the dementors', 'Mother, stop him', 'I'm your son!' - a sustained, anguished appeal that reveals a young man terrified and claiming innocence, creating moral ambiguity and pathos.
damaging_scenes.film_portrayal: Barty Jr. attempts to physically escape the courtroom, is stunned by Moody, restrained, brought before his father, flicks his tongue, and defiantly says 'Hello, father' - aggressive and defiant rather than desperate and pleading.
damaging_scenes.explanation: The book's Barty Jr. in this scene is defined by desperate pleading and claimed innocence, which creates moral complexity - the reader cannot be entirely sure of his guilt, and his father's cold rejection of a begging child is what makes Crouch Sr. monstrous. The film replaces this with cool defiance and the tongue-flick, collapsing the ambiguity and the emotional horror of the father-son dynamic into a straightforward villain reveal. The relationship damage between father and son is gutted: 'You are no son of mine' lands far less when the son is sneering rather than weeping.

damaging_scenes.scene: Film Scene 2 - Pensieve trial: 'I'm your son!' exchange
damaging_scenes.book_baseline: The climactic cry 'I'm your son! I'm your son!' followed by Crouch Sr.'s 'You are no son of mine! I have no son!' is the emotional and thematic peak of the scene - a father publicly disowning a begging child, defining both characters and the cost of Crouch Sr.'s fanatical devotion to law over family.
damaging_scenes.film_portrayal: The line 'You are no son of mine' is present but delivered to a defiant, tongue-flicking Barty Jr. who has just said 'Hello, father' with a smirk. The son does not cry out 'I'm your son' - the line is absent.
damaging_scenes.explanation: The absence of Barty Jr.'s 'I'm your son!' removes the thematic core of the scene. The book uses this moment to show that Crouch Sr.'s public righteousness destroys his own family - a theme that pays off later when Crouch Sr. is broken by guilt. Without the son's desperate claim of kinship, the father's rejection loses its cruelty and the scene's function as character-defining tragedy for both men is severely damaged.

damaging_scenes.scene: Film Scene 2 - Pensieve trial: tongue-flick as primary characterization
damaging_scenes.book_baseline: In the trial scene Barty Jr. is characterized entirely through voice and emotion - fear, desperation, grief, and the claim of innocence. The tongue-flick is a post-Azkaban tic associated with his time under Voldemort's influence and the Imperius Curse, not present during the original trial.
damaging_scenes.film_portrayal: Barty Jr. flicks his tongue during the trial itself, before any Azkaban imprisonment or Voldemort contact, using it as his primary distinguishing trait from his first appearance.
damaging_scenes.explanation: The tongue-flick in the trial scene is a continuity error that also flattens characterization: it signals 'villain' immediately, erasing the moral ambiguity of the trial scene and making Barty Jr. a straightforwardly sinister figure from the outset rather than a potentially wronged young man. It also undermines the tic's narrative function as a tell that 'Moody' is not who he claims to be.

damaging_scenes.scene: Film Scene 2 - Pensieve trial: Barty Jr.'s defiant line 'Get your filthy hands off of me, you pathetic little men'
damaging_scenes.book_baseline: Barty Jr. in the book does not speak defiantly at any point during the trial - he only pleads, weeps, and begs. His three companions are the ones who speak defiantly ('The Dark Lord will rise again, Crouch!').
damaging_scenes.film_portrayal: Barty Jr. shouts 'Get your filthy hands off of me, you pathetic little men!' when restrained - a line of contemptuous defiance that belongs tonally to his companions in the book.
damaging_scenes.explanation: This line transfers the defiant Death Eater voice from the other prisoners to Barty Jr., merging his characterization with theirs and eliminating the distinction the book carefully draws: his companions are true believers who go quietly and proudly, while he alone fights and weeps. This distinction is what makes his case morally interesting.

damaging_scenes.scene: Film Scene 3 - Unmasking: 'I'll be welcomed back like a hero'
damaging_scenes.book_baseline: The book's unmasked Barty Jr. under Veritaserum delivers a full confession explaining his history, his escape from Azkaban, his mother's sacrifice, his years under the Imperius Curse, and his devotion to Voldemort - a complete arc revelation that contextualizes everything.
damaging_scenes.film_portrayal: Barty Jr.'s unmasked dialogue is reduced to a few taunting lines: 'I'll show you mine if you show me yours', confirming Voldemort's return, and 'I'll be welcomed back like a hero' - triumphant and taunting with no backstory, no explanation of his escape, no mention of his mother.
damaging_scenes.explanation: The Veritaserum confession in the book is the payoff for the entire Barty Jr. arc - it explains how he escaped Azkaban (his mother died for him), how he was controlled and then freed, and why he is so fanatically devoted to Voldemort. Without it, his motivation is opaque, his mother's sacrifice is entirely absent, and the audience has no understanding of who he actually is beyond a sneering villain. This is the primary structural damage to the character.

damaging_scenes.scene: Film Scene 1 - World Cup: Barty Jr. casts Morsmordre silently and walks away
damaging_scenes.book_baseline: In the book, Barty Jr. (as the wand-stealer) casts the Dark Mark while Harry is unconscious nearby - a furtive, purposeful act. The book does not show the caster at all in the moment; his identity is the mystery.
damaging_scenes.film_portrayal: Barty Jr. is shown on screen casting 'Morsmordre' and walking away coolly, visible to the audience from the start of the film.
damaging_scenes.explanation: Revealing Barty Jr. as the caster this early removes the mystery that drives much of the book's tension around who conjured the Dark Mark. While the film must make visual choices, showing him openly collapses the whodunit element and reduces his role to a cool villain cameo rather than a hidden threat. Minor structural damage but meaningful loss of suspense architecture.
